 {"id":"bd-2ay73","title":"Add process name fallback for agent detection","description":"## Summary
When pane title doesn't match known agent patterns, fall back to querying the foreground process name to detect agents.

## Context
- GitHub Issue #20 reports agent detection fails when pane title changes dynamically
- Shell prompts and tmux hooks can change pane titles
- Need fallback detection mechanism

## Requirements
1. Primary: Check pane title against agent patterns (existing behavior)
2. Fallback: Query foreground process name via tmux
3. Cache results to avoid repeated lookups
4. Support all known agents: claude, codex, gemini-cli, aider, cursor

## Technical Approach
tmux list-panes -F '#{pane_current_command}' or tmux display-message -p -t PANE '#{pane_current_command}'

Process name patterns to detect:
- claude, claude-code -> Claude Code
- codex -> Codex CLI  
- gemini -> Gemini CLI
- aider -> Aider
- cursor -> Cursor

## Files to Modify
- internal/agent/patterns.go - Add process name patterns
- internal/robot/probe.go - Add fallback detection logic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Agent detected even when pane title is custom/dynamic
- [ ] No performance regression (cache process lookups)
- [ ] Existing title-based detection still works as primary
- [ ] Tests cover both title and process detection paths","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-23T05:02:29.215906059Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-23T05:07:30.081155970Z","closed_at":"2026-01-23T05:07:30.081113260Z","close_reason":"Implemented process name fallback in internal/tmux/session.go","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-2b0eb","title":"AccountRotator: Track account rotation history","description":"## Purpose
Track account rotation history per pane to enable analytics, debugging, and rate limit learning across sessions.

## Implementation Details

```go
// RotationHistory tracks all account rotations with persistence
type RotationHistory struct {
    dataDir   string
    history   map[string][]RotationRecord // pane -> records
    mu        sync.RWMutex
    logger    *slog.Logger
}

type RotationRecord struct {
    Timestamp       time.Time `json:"timestamp"`
    SessionPane     string    `json:"session_pane"`
    AgentType       string    `json:"agent_type"`
    Project         string    `json:"project"`
    PreviousAccount string    `json:"previous_account"`
    NewAccount      string    `json:"new_account"`
    TriggerPattern  string    `json:"trigger_pattern"`
    TimeSinceLast   time.Duration `json:"time_since_last,omitempty"`
}

// RecordRotation logs a rotation event
func (h *RotationHistory) RecordRotation(r RotationRecord) {
    h.mu.Lock()
    defer h.mu.Unlock()
    
    paneHistory := h.history[r.SessionPane]
    
    // Calculate time since last rotation for this pane
    if len(paneHistory) > 0 {
        last := paneHistory[len(paneHistory)-1]
        r.TimeSinceLast = r.Timestamp.Sub(last.Timestamp)
    }
    
    h.history[r.SessionPane] = append(paneHistory, r)
    
    h.logger.Info("rotation recorded",
        "session_pane", r.SessionPane,
        "agent_type", r.AgentType,
        "previous", r.PreviousAccount,
        "new", r.NewAccount,
        "time_since_last", r.TimeSinceLast,
        "total_rotations", len(h.history[r.SessionPane]))
}

// GetRotationStats returns statistics for analysis
func (h *RotationHistory) GetRotationStats(agentType string) RotationStats {
    h.mu.RLock()
    defer h.mu.RUnlock()
    
    var stats RotationStats
    for _, records := range h.history {
        for _, r := range records {
            if r.AgentType == agentType {
                stats.TotalRotations++
                stats.AvgTimeBetween += r.TimeSinceLast
                stats.AccountUsage[r.NewAccount]++
            }
        }
    }
    // ... calculate averages
    return stats
}

// SaveToFile persists history to .ntm/rotation_history.json
func (h *RotationHistory) SaveToFile() error {
    // Similar to ratelimit.RateLimitTracker.SaveToDir pattern
}
```

## Logging Requirements
- Log each rotation with full context
- Log time since last rotation for pattern analysis
- Log statistics on request (debug level)

## Integration with Existing Code
- Follow persistence pattern from internal/ratelimit/tracker.go
- Store in .ntm directory alongside rate_limits.json

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Track rotation records with timestamps
- [ ] Calculate time between rotations per pane
- [ ] Provide aggregate statistics per agent type
- [ ] Persist to disk in .ntm directory
- [ ] Load history on startup
- [ ] Thread-safe access with RWMutex
- [ ] Unit tests for stats calculations","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TopazCliff","created_at":"2026-01-22T07:01:53.149378960Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T22:06:14.813632604Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2b0eb","depends_on_id":"bd-3d4i9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:26Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2b0eb","depends_on_id":"bd-3lo54","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:26Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2b35","title":"EPIC: Session Profiles & Templates System","description":"**IDEA #12 - REDUCES DECISION FATIGUE (73% confidence)**

## What It Does
Pre-configured session layouts for common development patterns. One command to spawn a session optimized for code review, refactoring, greenfield development, etc.

## Why This Matters
- Currently: Users must decide agent counts and roles each time
- After: Common patterns are one command away
- Especially valuable for newcomers learning optimal configurations

## Built-in Templates
ntm spawn myproject --template=code-review
ntm spawn myproject --template=refactor
ntm spawn myproject --template=greenfield
ntm spawn myproject --template=bug-hunt
ntm spawn myproject --template=documentation

## Template Definition Format (templates.toml)
[[templates]]
name = 'code-review'
description = 'Optimized for reviewing PRs and providing feedback'
agents = { cc = 2, cod = 1 }
personas = ['reviewer', 'implementer', 'tester']
initial_prompts = [
    { target = 'cc', message = 'You are a code reviewer...' },
    { target = 'cod', message = 'You will implement fixes...' }
]

## Success Criteria

1. Built-in templates for 5 common workflows
2. Custom template definition via TOML files
3. ntm spawn --template NAME works correctly
4. ntm templates list shows available templates
5. Templates include agent configs and prompts
6. Templates stored in config directory
7. Unit tests for template parsing (80% coverage)
8. E2E tests verify template-based spawning","notes":"## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: core logic, error paths, edge cases.
- E2E scripts: full CLI/robot flow with detailed log/slog prefixes (e.g., [E2E-<AREA>]) and include session/agent/bead IDs.
- If structured outputs are affected, assert schema stability + deterministic ordering.

## Test Coverage

- Test version parsing from various formats
- Test shell alias detection (zsh, bash)
- Test plugin detection (oh-my-zsh tmux plugin)

### Session Structure Tests
- Test window index detection
- Test pane index range parsing
- Test control vs agent pane identification
- Test empty session handling

### Shell Environment Tests
- Test shell type detection (zsh, bash, fish)
- Test oh-my-zsh detection
- Test config path discovery
- Test plugin enumeration

### Timing Tests
- Test default values returned
- Test config file override
- Test environment variable override
- Test priority order (config > env > default)

### Targeting Tests
- Test example generation correctness
- Test format string accuracy
- Test various session names (with special chars)

## Mocking Strategy

- Mock which/type commands
- Mock tmux list-* output
- Mock file system reads for config detection
- Mock environment variables

## Logging Requirements

- Mock configuration at test start
- Command outputs being simulated
- Expected vs actual values

 {"id":"bd-3exkg","title":"E2E Tests: Robot Probe","description":"E2E tests for Robot Probe with actual tmux sessions.

## Test Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Responsive Agent (keystroke_echo)
Spawn session, wait for agent initialization, execute probe, verify responsive=true

### Scenario 2: Unresponsive Pane (shell prompt)
Create session with just shell (no agent), execute probe, verify shell responds

### Scenario 3: Hung Process Simulation
Spawn session, make pane unresponsive with infinite loop, probe should detect unresponsive

### Scenario 4: Method Comparison
Test keystroke_echo vs interrupt_test methods, compare results

### Scenario 5: Custom Timeout
Test short (100ms) and long (10s) timeouts, verify behavior changes

### Scenario 6: Aggressive Mode Fallback
Test aggressive mode falls back to interrupt_test if keystroke_echo fails

### Scenario 7: Invalid Pane
Test with pane 999, verify PANE_NOT_FOUND error

### Scenario 8: Integration with Diagnose
Verify probe and diagnose results are consistent

## Logging Requirements
Each test logs: scenario name, setup params, probe params, result JSON, assertions, pass/fail, duration_ms

## Acceptance Criteria

1. All 8 test scenarios implemented as bash scripts
2. Each scenario logs full JSON responses
3. Assertions verify specific JSON fields
4. Cleanup removes all test sessions
5. Tests are idempotent (can run multiple times)
6. Exit code 0 on all pass, non-zero on any failure
7. Total test suite runs in under 5 minutes","acceptance_criteria":"1. All 8 test scenarios pass
2. Test logs follow JSON format specification
3. Cleanup removes all test sessions
4. Tests are idempotent (can run multiple times)
5. Test duration under 5 minutes total
6. Each scenario logs setup/execute/verify/result
7. Exit code 0 on all pass, 1 on any failure
8. -{"id":"bd-3ffb","title":"Add ntm send --dry-run to preview what would be sent to each agent without sending","description":"## Overview
Add --dry-run flag to ntm send to preview what would be sent to each agent without actually sending.

## Problem
Users want to verify prompt targeting before committing to sends, especially with complex targeting rules.

## Solution
`ntm send myproject --dry-run` shows exactly what would be sent without sending.

## Output Format
### Human Mode
```
Dry Run: ntm send myproject --batch prompts.txt

Would send 5 prompts:
  1. cc_1: "Implement feature A" (from line 1)
  2. cc_2: "Implement feature B" (from line 5)
  3. cod_1: "Write tests for A" (from line 9)
  4. cod_2: "Write tests for B" (from line 13)
  5. gmi_1: "Review documentation" (from line 17)

Use without --dry-run to execute.
```

### Robot Mode
```json
{
  "dry_run": true,
  "would_send": [
    {"pane": 1, "agent": "cc_1", "prompt": "Implement...", "source": "line:1"},
    {"pane": 2, "agent": "cc_2", "prompt": "Implement...", "source": "line:5"}
  ],
  "total": 5
}
```

## CLI Examples
```bash
# Preview batch send
ntm send myproject --batch prompts.txt --dry-run

# Preview with targeting
ntm send myproject --msg "Review code" --type cc --dry-run

# Preview assign
ntm assign myproject --dry-run
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No prompts actually sent in dry-run
- [ ] Output shows exact targeting
- [ ] Works with all send variants
- [ ] Robot mode JSON output
- [ ] Shows source (line number, bead ID)

## Tests Required
- Unit test: Dry-run flag prevents sending
- Unit test: Output format correctness
- E2E test: Dry-run matches actual send targeting","notes":"## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: core logic, error paths, edge cases.
- E2E scripts: full CLI/robot flow with detailed log/slog prefixes (e.g., [E2E-<AREA>]) and include session/agent/bead IDs.
- If structured outputs are affected, assert schema stability + deterministic ordering.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:36:12.126264994Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T21:39:09.677226593Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ffb","depends_on_id":"bd-10ufs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3ffb","depends_on_id":"bd-3kmf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3fg81","title":"External Project Integration Initiative","description":"Master EPIC for integrating 9 external Rust/Go projects into NTM to create a comprehensive multi-agent orchestration platform with safety, performance, observability, and knowledge management capabilities.

## Background & Strategic Vision

NTM (Named Tmux Manager) is a Go-based multi-agent orchestration platform that manages Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini agents in tmux panes. It provides ~60 robot mode JSON commands for AI agent automation, a TUI with Charmbracelet, command palette, and dashboard.

The 9 external projects represent mature, battle-tested tools that can dramatically enhance NTM's capabilities:

### Tier 1: Safety & Reliability (Immediate Priority)
- **DCG** (destructive_commit_guard): Blocks dangerous commands like rm -rf, git reset --hard
- **CAAM** (coding_agent_account_manager): OAuth vault with <100ms account switching for rate limit recovery
- **caut** (coding_agent_usage_tracker): Real-time quota/spend tracking across 16+ LLM providers

### Tier 2: Performance Optimizations
- **TOON** (toon_rust): Token-Optimized Object Notation - 40-60% token reduction vs JSON
- **RCH** (remote_compilation_helper): Offload compilation to remote workers, prevent CPU storms

### Tier 3: Observability Layer
- **rano**: Network observer for AI CLI processes - per-agent API call tracking
- **process_triage**: Bayesian classification of useful/abandoned/zombie processes

### Tier 4: Knowledge & Infrastructure
- **xf**: Hybrid search (BM25+semantic) for X/Twitter archives
- **rust_proxy**: Transparent iptables-based proxy with automatic failover

## Success Criteria
1. All Tier 1 integrations complete and tested
2. Robot mode commands added for each integration
3. Dashboard widgets for observability integrations
4. Comprehensive documentation in README.md
5. All integrations follow NTM's pure Go architecture (wrappers/FFI as needed)

## Architectural Principles
- NTM remains pure Go - integrate via CLI subprocess, IPC, or FFI
- Robot mode output must remain parseable (JSON or TOON)
- Each integration should be toggleable via config/flags
- Fail-safe: integrations must degrade gracefully if external tool unavailable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-21T06:41:23.616076852Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T06:42:42.730744833Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":27,"issue_id":"bd-3fg81","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implementation Roadmap & Rationale

### Why This Order?

**Tier 1 First (DCG → CAAM → caut)**:
- DCG provides immediate safety net with zero code changes needed in agents
- CAAM enables automatic recovery from rate limits (common pain point)
- caut gives visibility into spend before it becomes a problem

**Tier 2 Second (TOON → RCH)**:
- TOON reduces token costs across all robot mode interactions (compounds)
- RCH prevents CPU storms during parallel builds (common in multi-agent)

**Tier 3 Third (rano → process_triage)**:
- Both observability tools - see what's happening inside the swarm
- rano: network-level visibility (API calls, latency)
- process_triage: process-level health (stuck agents)

**Tier 4 Last (xf → rust_proxy)**:
- Nice-to-haves that enhance specific workflows
- xf: knowledge retrieval (useful but not critical path)
- rust_proxy: infrastructure layer (complex, can work without)

### Risk Mitigation

1. **FFI Complexity**: Rust integrations may need Go wrappers or subprocess calls
2. **Performance**: Subprocess overhead for real-time commands (mitigate with caching)
3. **Versioning**: External tools may update independently (pin versions, test CI)
4. **Config Sprawl**: Many new options (consolidate into [integrations] TOML section)

### Dependencies Between Projects

- TOON output requires caut to parse TOON (update caut if needed)
- rust_proxy can wrap CAAM OAuth calls for additional routing
- process_triage benefits from rano network data (correlation)
- DCG should whitelist RCH remote commands","created_at":"2026-01-21T06:42:42Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3fif","title":"Task: Implement audit logger with append-only storage","description":"## Overview
Create the core audit logging infrastructure with immutable, append-only storage.

## Deliverables
1. Create internal/audit/logger.go:
   - AuditLogger struct
   - Log(entry AuditEntry) error
   - Close() error - flush and close log file

2. AuditEntry struct:
   - Timestamp: RFC3339 time
   - SessionID: NTM session identifier
   - EventType: command, spawn, send, response, error, state_change
   - Actor: user, agent, system
   - Target: what was affected
   - Payload: event-specific data (map[string]any)
   - Metadata: correlation_id, request_id, etc.
   - Checksum: SHA256 of entry (for tamper evidence)

3. Storage format:
   - JSONL (one JSON object per line)
   - File per session: ~/.local/share/ntm/audit/<session>-<date>.jsonl
   - Append-only (no modifications allowed)
   - File locking for concurrent access

4. Tamper evidence:
   - Each entry includes hash of previous entry
   - Enables verification of log integrity
   - Detect if entries were removed or modified

## Implementation Notes
- Buffer writes for performance (flush every N entries or M seconds)
- Handle disk full gracefully
- Compress old logs automatically

## Acceptance Criteria
- Entries written in correct format
- Append-only enforced
- Checksums validate correctly
- Concurrent writes work safely
- Unit tests for logging and validation","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T18:32:59.785548538Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-20T01:52:53.521364836Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T01:52:53.521322086Z","close_reason":"Completed audit logger implementation with tamper-evident hash chains, concurrent write safety, and comprehensive test coverage","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3fif","depends_on_id":"bd-3pbe","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3fl3r","title":"Task: Unit tests for new tool adapters","description":"## Overview
Add unit tests for new flywheel tool adapters (DCG, SLB, RU, MS, UBS, ACFS, GIIL, XF, JFP).

## Tests
- Detect() returns false when binary missing
- Version() parsing handles common version formats
- Health() returns healthy/unhealthy with clear message
- Timeouts handled (no hangs)

## Logging
Use `t.Logf("TOOLS_TEST: tool=%s installed=%t", name, installed)` and include version strings in logs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests exist for each new adapter
- Tests run without the external tools installed (mock exec)
- Contract tests in `internal/tools/contract_test.go` updated as needed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T19:29:08.402829551Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-20T06:33:03.867248045Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T06:33:03.867157916Z","close_reason":"All unit tests in place: version parsing tests for SLB, ACFS, MS, GIIL; contract tests for all 14 adapters (bv, bd, cass, cm, s2p, am, jfp, dcg, slb, acfs, ms, giil, ru, xf); fake tools created for all adapters. All tests pass.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-1v52p","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-25kxv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-2cz9y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-2iiv4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-3kfv0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-3kt42","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-3pqjq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-lzs23","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-v0ran","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-3fl3r","depends_on_id":"bd-z23js","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-30T23:34:28Z","created_by":"import"}]}
